result.markdown:
# The Rental Market {#sec-market-rental} ## Who rents ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Warrenton is the county's clearest concentration of renters](market-rental_files/figure-html/fig-renter-share-1.png){#fig-renter-share fig-align='left' fig-alt='Horizontal bar chart of the renter share of occupied households in 2024 for Warrenton, Fauquier County, Bealeton, and Virginia. Warrenton is about 37% renter, well above Fauquier County and Bealeton at roughly 21% each; Virginia sits at about 33%. Renter household counts are labeled on each bar.' width=100%} ::: ::: - **Fauquier is overwhelmingly an ownership county** — only about 21% of households rent, below Virginia's 33%. - **Warrenton is the exception:** roughly 37% of the town's 3,985 households rent — nearly double the county rate and the county's principal rental submarket. - **Bealeton tracks the county** (~21% renter); its renter count is small and carries Medium reliability, so it is read as a count, not a precise rate. ## What renters pay ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Rents have climbed in real terms; professional asking rents sit at the top of the market](market-rental_files/figure-html/fig-rent-trend-1.png){#fig-rent-trend fig-align='left' fig-alt='Line chart of Fauquier County median gross rent from 2013 to 2024 shown nominal and inflation-adjusted, with CoStar professional asking rents overlaid quarterly from 2016 to 2026. Nominal median rent rises from about $1,140 to $1,610; the real line is roughly flat-to-up; CoStar adjusted asking rent hovers near $1,550–$1,600, at or just above the ACS median. Town points for Warrenton and Bealeton sit near the county line.' width=100%} ::: ::: - The county's **median gross rent rose from $1,138 (2013) to $1,613 (2024)** — **+42% nominal, +10% in real terms** — so renters have lost ground even after inflation. - **CoStar professional asking rents (~$1,604, adjusted) sit at or just above the ACS median**, consistent with a small stock of newer market-rate apartments pricing above the broader rental pool. - **Town rents track the county:** all Warrenton and Bealeton rent cells are High reliability, and both towns' 2024 medians sit within roughly $50–$200 of the county figure. ## Where the rental stock prices ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Most rentals now price at $1,500 or more](market-rental_files/figure-html/fig-rent-distribution-1.png){#fig-rent-distribution fig-align='left' fig-alt='Grouped bar chart of the gross rent distribution for cash-rent renters in Fauquier County, Warrenton, and Bealeton across six rent bands. The $1,500–$1,999 band is the largest in the county at roughly a quarter of renters, and shares rise sharply above $1,000; bands under $1,000 are thin. Town bars with too few sample cases are omitted.' width=100%} ::: ::: - The **modal rent band is $1,500–1,999** (about 25% of county cash-rent renters); combined with the bands above it, **most renters now pay $1,500 or more**. - **Sub-$1,000 rentals are scarce** — only about 14% of the county's renters — underscoring how little of the stock is affordable to lower-income households. - Place-level distributions are noisy; only the higher-count Warrenton bands survive the reliability screen, and they mirror the county's upward concentration. ## The professional apartment market ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![A small, tight professional apartment market with steadily rising rents](market-rental_files/figure-html/fig-costar-1.png){#fig-costar fig-align='left' fig-alt='Two-panel time-series chart of Fauquier County\'s professional multifamily market from 2016 to 2026. The top panel shows nominal asking rent rising from about $1,060 to $1,600 per unit while the inflation-adjusted line is roughly flat near $1,550–$1,600. The bottom panel shows the vacancy rate falling from about 5% to near 1% around 2019–2021, then edging back up to roughly 2–3%.' width=100%} ::: ::: - The professional market is **tiny: about 998 market-rate units across 14 CoStar-tracked apartment properties** (roughly 41 multifamily properties appear in the broader CoStar inventory). - **Asking rent reached ~$1,603/unit (nominal, latest quarter)**; in constant 2025 dollars asking rents are roughly flat near **$1,604** — professional operators have kept pace with, not outrun, inflation. - **Vacancy is very low** — recently about 2.8%, and under ~3% for most of the past decade — a persistently tight market with little slack. ## Rental stock by structure type ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Most of Fauquier's rental stock is single-family homes, not apartments](market-rental_files/figure-html/fig-rental-structure-1.png){#fig-rental-structure fig-align='left' fig-alt='Horizontal bar chart of Fauquier County\'s renter-occupied housing units by structure type. Single-family detached homes are the largest share at about 57% of rentals, followed by 20-plus-unit buildings and 5-to-19-unit buildings; single-family detached and attached together make up roughly two-thirds of the rental stock.' width=100%} ::: ::: - **Single-family homes are the backbone of the rental market:** detached and attached houses together are about **68% of all renter-occupied units** — single-family detached alone is ~57%. - **Purpose-built apartments (5+ units) are only ~20% of rentals** — the mirror image of @fig-costar's thin professional market, and why so much of the rental supply behaves like the for-sale market. - The reliance on scattered single-family rentals means renters compete for the same limited housing stock as buyers; place-level structure detail is too small to size reliably, so this is shown county-wide. ## Fair Market Rents vs. actual rents ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Metro Fair Market Rents run well above what Fauquier renters actually pay](market-rental_files/figure-html/fig-fmr-1.png){#fig-fmr fig-align='left' fig-alt='Dot-and-range chart comparing rents by bedroom size in Fauquier County. For one- through four-bedroom units, the HUD metro-area Fair Market Rent sits far above the ACS actual median gross rent; the Small Area FMR range across the three town ZIP codes falls between the two but still above the local median. The gap is largest for one- and two-bedroom units.' width=100%} ::: ::: - Because Fauquier is folded into the high-cost **Washington, D.C. metro FMR area, the county's 2BR FMR ($2,246) sits far above the actual ACS median 2BR rent ($1,437)** — a gap of roughly $800. - **Small Area FMRs pull the benchmark toward local reality** but still land above the ACS median at every bedroom size: the town-ZIP 2BR SAFMR ranges $1,520–$2,340, with Bealeton (22712) lowest. - The practical implication is favorable for voucher holders — generous payment standards — but it means **FMRs overstate the going market and are a poor gauge of local affordability**; the ACS medians (@fig-rent-trend) are the better yardstick. ## Assisted inventory & preservation risk ::: {#tbl-nhpd .cell layout-align="left" tbl-cap='Federally assisted rental properties, sorted by earliest subsidy expiration'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Property City Units Assisted units Programs Earliest expiration
Oaks Ii Warrenton 96 111 9% Tax Credit Jan 2027
Highland Commons Warrenton 96 96 9% Tax Credit Jan 2031
Countryside Bealeton Bealeton 8 8 9% Tax Credit Jan 2031
Aspen Village Bealeton 108 138 4% Tax Credit, 9% Tax Credit Jan 2032
Warrenton Manor Warrenton 98 166 9% Tax Credit, HFDA/8 NC Jun 2033
Hunt Country Manor Warrenton 56 55 4% Tax Credit Jan 2034
Academy Hill Warrenton 31 31 515 Rural Housing Jul 2034
Millview Remington 28 39 9% Tax Credit, HOME May 2036
Moffett Manor Warrenton 98 98 9% Tax Credit Jan 2038
Remington Group Home Remington 6 6 PRAC/811 Mar 2038
Piedmont Lane At The Plains The Plains 16 16 9% Tax Credit Jan 2043
Washburn Place Marshall 30 30 9% Tax Credit Jan 2047
Mintbrook Senior Apartments Bealeton 80 80 9% Tax Credit Jan 2048
**Source:** National Housing Preservation Database (NHPD). Assisted-unit counts may exceed total units where a property carries overlapping subsidies. Programs list active subsidy types per property.
````` ::: ::: - Fauquier's assisted stock is **874 assisted units across 13 federally supported properties** — a small cushion against the market rents documented above. - **Preservation risk is real and near-term:** the soonest subsidy expiration is January 2027, and **8 of the 13 properties** have a subsidy expiring by 2036 — the window in which preservation decisions must be made. - The **program mix is dominated by Low-Income Housing Tax Credit** deals, with a handful of older project-based Section 8, Section 202, and USDA Rural Housing (515) properties whose subsidies are the closest to expiring. ::: {.callout-note} ## Town spotlight — assisted units concentrate in and around Warrenton Most of the county's assisted properties sit in **Warrenton** (and nearby Remington), the same submarket that carries the county's highest renter share (@fig-renter-share). Bealeton has little to no dedicated assisted inventory in the NHPD record — so the affordable options there are effectively limited to whatever scattered single-family and market-rate units price low, which @fig-rent-distribution shows is a shrinking share. ::: ## What the interviews said vs. what the data shows ::: {.callout-important} ## Interview validation - **"Only about 2 of 19 available rentals were priced under $1,700 (spring 2026)."** *Confirmed and nuanced.* In 2025 the MLS recorded **28 of 203 closed leases under $1,700** (about 14%), with a **median lease of $2,450** — a somewhat larger affordable share than the single snapshot suggested, but the same story: sub-$1,700 rentals are scarce and most of the market prices well above (@fig-rent-distribution, @fig-rent-trend). - **"There aren't enough apartments — most rentals are just houses."** *Confirmed.* Purpose-built apartments (5+ units) are only ~20% of the rental stock and the professional market spans just 14 tracked properties (@fig-rental-structure, @fig-costar); single-family homes carry ~68% of all rentals. - **"Affordable, subsidized units are limited and aging."** *Confirmed and nuanced.* Only 874 assisted units exist county-wide (@tbl-nhpd), concentrated around Warrenton, and 8 of 13 assisted properties face subsidy expiration within about a decade — a preservation challenge layered on top of an already thin affordable supply. :::
